turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in number oh oh twelve fourteen, Alabama versus LeReed Shelton. General Pryor. [Inaudible]

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. Thirty years ago in Argersinger versus Hamlin and then more than twenty years ago in Scott versus Illinois, this Court established the principle that, under the Sixth and Fourteenth Amendments, a State is not obligated to provide an indigent defendant in a misdemeanor case court appointed and taxpayer funded counsel, provided that the defendant is not actually imprisoned upon conviction. Eight years ago, in Nichols versus the United States, this Court reaffirmed that principle. The Supreme Court of Alabama distorted this well established and workable rule and held that a probated or suspended sentence, which actually liberates a defendant to return to free society, nevertheless triggers a right to court appointed and taxpayer funded counsel. There're three arguments that I would like to address...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: this mo- Do you...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: do you concede that the State can never impose the original sentence of time in jail?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Your Honor, obviously that is not a court a a question that this Court has directly addressed in either Argersinger or Scott.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I'm asking whether the State of Alabama concedes that it can't ever impose that original sentence.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Our reading of of Scott is that th- that we cannot activate the suspended sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the court...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: what happens in Alabama if if a a criminal defendant is convicted of a misdemeanor and placed on probation and then violates probation? Does that enable the State to impose the original sentence for violation of the probation?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Yes, ordinarily it would.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, then we're jumping probably ahead into wh- what you're going to tell us, but while we're at this point,

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Right...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: It It It seems to me that if i- if you say that the s- sentence cannot be reim- im- imposed, you're saying that the State courts are in the position of imposing a sentence th- th- that is s- s- s- s- s- s- s- s- something of a ruse.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Well well, there's still a d- a risk of imprisonment, the same risk of imprisonment, under our reading of of Argersinger and Scott, that that is accompanied with the judgment of a mere fine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: you're you're treating the contempt proceeding, in effect, as a separate proceeding then, and I take it, though I'm not sure that I remember this from your briefs, I take it that on on your view of the way the scheme ought to operate in the contempt proceeding, before there could be any confinement on a finding of contempt, that counsel would have to be provided then if if the individual is indigent and didn't waive it.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That That's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Souter.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: counseled proceeding that distinguishes your case from from the case that Mr. Fried argues for.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That that's correct, Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: appointed...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: from your point of view, General Pryor, what does the State gain by, as as opposed to what the Supreme Court of Alabama said, by following following your your line of reasoning? as I think as Justice Kennedy said, it it it imposes a sentence, but a sentence that everybody knows can't be enforced.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: The State gains the powerful tool of probation to rehabilitate an offender that the State believes is a good risk, a risk to return to free society who can be rehabilitated, d- depending on whatever mechanisms of probation have been adopted by the trial court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: dangerous offenders and...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: provide counsel in more serious cases.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But wh- what w- ho- how effective is probation going to be if there isn't the threat of of a of a sentence in case of probation violation?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: I I I think, at a minimum, the the probation is going to be as effective as a judgment of a fine because the State is going to still have the flexible power of contempt, whether civil or criminal in nature, to ensure that its orders are followed and and will continue to exercise jurisdiction of the probationer during that period of...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: probation.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: is it the case in Alabama that probation cannot be imposed without a suspended sentence?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice S-

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: wouldn't we have at least a more candid system or wouldn't the interaction of of Argersinger and and the Alabama system, as you view Argersinger, produce a more candid system if if we took the position that, no, they can't impose a suspended sentence, and Alabama would then presumably amend its laws so that probation could be imposed without imposing a suspended sentence, and you, on your view, would have your your contempt remedy.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: W- Alabama certainly has the freedom to adopt, I think, either system without running afoul of the Constitution, which is th- the issue before this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the mechanics of of it...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: is there any State which imposes probation without a suspended sentence that you know of?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: I know from the amicus brief of the National Association of Criminal Defense Lawyers that there are some States that impose, in fact, pretrial probation, that that is something that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: post-trial, after a conviction?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: I I just don't know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: General Pryor, I'm s-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: would the State have to to put somebody on probation un- unless unless it is the suspension of of a judgment of incarceration? I mean, can a State just go around saying you're going to be on probation?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to me the only the only reason it it it has that grip over the person is that th- is that th- it has a right to incarcerate him.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: the States certainly view that as an effective mechanism...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in most...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm not sure there's an alternative to it.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: I think that the that the Alabama legislature has the inherent power to define what a sentence is, whether a sentence is a fine, or whether a sentence is imprisonment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: take it what they would do on that scheme would be to say, upon conviction of offense A, the court may impose probation, and if the conditions of probation are violated and are shown in a separate trial or proceeding to that effect, the violation itself can be punished.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: if if Alabama wanted it.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: If Alabama wanted it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: General Pryor, explain to me how some other States approach it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It's a...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: deferred prosecution on condition that the the defendant abide by certain terms and conditions.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That's That's correct.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And if if the defendant then doesn't live up to it, then it proceeds to trial.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Then it proceeds...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to trial.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: States use a system like...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: believe the the b- the brief listed twenty-three States.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, these are pretrial diversion programs which have been very helpful in the drug context, but they're extremely expensive to administer.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: have the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that any more unseemly than the ordinary plea bargain?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Because at least in the in the in the case of the ordinary plea bargain, the defendant comes forward and admits the wrongdoing.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But She- Shelton was not given any pretrial diversion.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, and Alabama doesn't have it in this context except with respect to drug offenders.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: General Pryor, I can understand a line between a fine and any kind of confinement, and probation may involve no immediate incarceration, but it does involve what could be very significant restraints on a person.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: W- Justice Ginsburg, I'd say no for a couple of reasons.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Under your view, if you enforced the jail sentence through the contempt mechanism and reached the same result, I I take it you now have a second jury trial.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Well, I I guess it would be dependent in part on the nature of the contempt proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: For more than twenty...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: then but then the contempt is is is is is really not substitute of an equivalent for the pro- f- f- for the imposition of the suspended sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the suspended sent-

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That's absolutely right, although I I would argue that that the threat of imprisonmenent [: imprisonment] is still enough to give the probationer an incentive to follow the orders of the court.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, General Pryor.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: [NS] Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think it is...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: is that a red herring? Because I y- s- I got from your brief the impression that the the there's a practical problem that that a lot of States do without giving people lawyers.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: It do- it does work.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: recognized.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: you do allow the possibility that counsel could be provided in the event the probation is revoked.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I think that's a very real possibility.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: change...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: the rev- revocation hearing would not necessarily be whether he committed a crime.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: something. And he'd get...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: counsel to defend him against that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: the crime itself.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, it would als- it would also cover, I take it, whether or not the plea was voluntarieen [: voluntary] and knowingly made originally.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: That could be brought up.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It would not...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: that at the probation hearing you reexamine the validity of the original offense,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the original conviction?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Alabama is a good example of what is open at probation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: depreciating...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: alternatives assume the original conviction is valid.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I don't see why in those...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: proceedings, the lawyer...

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: could not argue, look, you're asking whether this will depreciate the gravity of the original offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: sentence.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: all pretty speculative, isn't it? Since that question doesn't confront us here?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: No question.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Do we have any idea of the one point four million or to one point eight million people who are on probation for misdemeanors, of what percentage of those cases the the probation of the defendant was not offered a lawyer? I mean,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the relevant feature...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: just that that's a rather,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: yeah.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: We try to infer that in the brief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: attention.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Fried, and thank you for your participation as an amicus.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: [NS] Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Do you agree that neither of those cases squarely control the outcome here?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I- i- it would be my position that Argersinger does by the some of the pronouncements that it makes.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Certainly the holding does not.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: The holding does not certainly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: include...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Court basically said, fine only? Okay.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, that that puzzled me somewhat when the State made that concession in its reply brief that this was in e- in effect a sham sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: court to make...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: case, what do we think about this?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Perhaps we're we're dealing with a sham sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: can't be enforced.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: clarify one thing for me? Did the Alabama Supreme Court endorse that view, or is that the Attorney General?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That's the Attorney General's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: view.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: What the Alabama appellate court and Supreme Court did was merely strike the the sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: One of the...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: not is a matter of State law, isn't it? I mean I I don't know what the remedy should be for a sentence that's not not an enforceable sentence and the person writes it in the the judge writes in the book, suspended sentence.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, I believe the Alabama Supreme Court said it was it was a matter of Federal law.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That it be struck rather than just li- l- l- l- l- left to lie there unenforceable.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I'm not not sure they articulated it in those terms, but their their basis for reaching the decision they reached was the the Federal cases that deal with the right of counsel.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: One of the things that didn't happen, we know for sure, in this case was that th- the- a- an an offer of counsel.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Perhaps it was inadvertence, but the the Alabama rules of criminal procedure ha- have that a- as a part of the th- the processing of an- any case.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: In in any misdemeanor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Any criminal...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: prosecution, yes, sir.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: N- no- not in those terms.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: rules...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: say where constitutionally required.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: So, they're not pr- providing counsel in these cases now because, I take it, the State's position is it's not constitutionally required.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: At what point do they regard it as being constitutionally required? When the sentence can exceed six months or what?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: getting at is I want to know I want your response to the argument that there is going to be a a great practical difference if we say, no, you may not impose sentences like this at all, even though they're merely suspended.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: From the th- the wording of the Alabama Rules of Criminal Procedure about appointment of counsel at the present time, I I would assume that the trial judge must go through the process outlined in Argersinger, that is, make the pretrial determination of whether imprisonment is a likely punishment in this particular case, and if if so, appoint counsel.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, any imprisonment? One day of imprisonment?

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Isn't that what...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the Federal what Argersinger requires?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Okay. But Alabama,

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: in other words, is not doing something eccentric in this respect.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That that's correct.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: The the only difference then between the cases in which c- misdemeanor cases, for example, in which counsel is offered, if an individual is indigent, and counsel is not offered are cases in which the judge says in advance I'm not going to put this guy away at all even if we convict him.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Presumably that's th- the process that's being followed.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Now, do you have do you have any basis to tell us what practical difference it would make if the judge m- said, well, I may impose a suspended sentence and therefore I will have to offer counsel because I read Argersinger as requiring that? Do you know what difference that would make in practical terms? How many cases would counsel have to be offered and pro- and potentially provided for where it's not being offered and potentially provided for now?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I don't have any data that would would support that, but it but it would seem that if the trial judge makes the pretrial determination that any sentence, wheth- whether immediate or suspended, is warranted or maybe warranted in this particular case, that that counsel should be...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: appointed. Of course, that's the position...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That's the position.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Mills, there is one thing that I think is a piece of information that that is in the record, and that is that most non-indigent misdeme- misdemeanors appear in court without counsel.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That perhaps is correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: True also isn't it true also, at least according to some of the statistics quoted in the that frequently these cases are not prosecuted by lawyers? They're simply plo- prosecuted by the arresting officer.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That happens occasionally in in Alabama.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: it would be constitutional for the State to offer i- the defendant at the outset the promise of imposition of a suspended sentence if counsel were waived?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: [NS] If If kn- knowingly waived, I I don't see a a constitutional problem with that.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Is that done sometimes in Alabama?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I- it it is.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: It it is done.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: And, of of course, this is th- this is the distinction between the the pretrial diversion mechanism that was addressed earlier an- and a trial.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, pu- put put in its raw form, if the judge said, now, if if you agree not to have a counsel, I'll agree not to impose a jail sentence, th- that that wouldn't be permitted.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Yes, sir, and and it seems if...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I assume.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I assume it r- r- correct me if I'm wrong.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I think that's correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: The The jail sentence would not be for the original crime necessarily but perhaps just for breaking probation.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, I I couldn't speak to all jurisdictions, but certainly un- under Alabama law, there would be no n- no way to impose a sentence for breaking probation.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, tha- tha- tha- that's what I mean.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: This, course, is correct in that this th- this is the the carrot and and and stick analogy which which...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the S- the State's b-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: misunderstood General Pryor's argument, but I thought he was here arguing no, he wouldn't impose the original sentence ever.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I think that's what he he said.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: because he quit his job then since...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: involve imposition of the original sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That was...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: what I understood as...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That would be something else.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That that is that is for the contempt it- itself.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: What is your position in the case of the individual who refuses to pay the fine? No suspended sentence of incarceration, simply a sentence of a fine, and he refuses to pay it.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: I think this Court has addressed that issue that it constitutionally can, and I I assume there's no no impediment to that under Alabama law.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, does is there is is there some tension between the position you take about the inability of the State to enforce a a condition of sentence or a condition of suspension and the position that you take or acknowledge about the ability to enforce the the sentence of fine? Why should the two cases be different?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, I think th- the th- th- the difference is if if the suspended sentence is imposed, a person is being convic- imprisoned because he committed a crime.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That would never...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: the fine is imposed, the fine is being imposed because the individual committed a crime.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, th- this is true, but only if he willfully refuses to pay it wh- where he has the ability to do so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well,

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: is the is the difference that you're making willful? That that violations of the conditions of suspension are not willful and refusals to pay fine are willful?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, I believe willfulness willfully refusing to pay the fine would be a a precondition to to a contempt sentence.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: That that's...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But that's just as that the the willfulness factor is the same in the refusal to abide by that condition as it is in the refusal to pay the fine.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, the the the fine and the the w- contempt for w- willfully refusing to comply with some other condition, I I I could not distinguish those.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Of course, l- let let me add one other thing about the contempt process to to enforce, u- under Alabama law, wouldn't save the State any money.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But But it would only it would only undergo that expense where there has been a breach of the conditions of of probation.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. MILLS
turns.text: Well, I w- I would say one th- per- perhaps the the number of cases would not be as large as as might be anticipated.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Mills.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court, were this a petty offense prosecution, perhaps the only legitimate, appropriate question would be the one that has occupied the Court thus far this morning, but this is not a petty offense prosecution.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: You're You're not saying it would guarantee counsel if if only a fine were imposed.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, how do you reconcile that with Argens- Argersinger?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Because Argersinger, Mr. Chief Justice, was a petty offense case.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, the but this wasn't the basis on y- the basis you're sus- s- su- submitting now wasn't the basis on which the Supreme Court of Alabama ruled, was it?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: It It was not the the rationale, but it w- it does support the result of the Alabama Supreme...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Court's decision.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: thought that the main issue that we had here is the one we've been talking about, is you have to give a person a lawyer when it is a petty offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I mean, and I...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: and I thought that that Mr. Fried's main point was don't do it because if you say you have to give a person a lawyer, where it's a petty offense and the only key thing is is a suspended sentence, I'll tell you there are a million, eight hundred thousand people who have received probation in petty offenses.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: S-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So, you might know if that's really so if o- or which seems hard to get at, how many of those one point five million walking around probation people did were never offered a lawyer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Wh- What's We don't know. So, what are we supposed...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: to do?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: The the the studies that I've seen suggest that in misdemeanor petty misdemeanor cases, re- frequently the the people are offered a lawyer in a in a in a group and s- it's suggested that most of them don't need lawyers because they're not going to jail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: not petty.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: petty offense because?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Because the authorized sentence in this case was one year, which made the jury trial right guaranteed him a right to a jury trial under the Sixth Amendment, and s- because this was a criminal prosecution.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But the the question presented is this.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Well, I I submit it does, Mr. Chief Justice, because as I suggested, Argersinger de- dealt with a petty offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Both of Our cas- our cases have d- have...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: r- r- reached different results on the on those two issues.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: But, Mr. Chief Justice, the the Scott opinion, which you wrote, did not actually address the serious offense six month distinction because the parties were not addressing it.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Where do you get that from, that you're appealing to the text of the Constitution.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: All...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: all misdemeanors criminal...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: offenses?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: criminal offenses but...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: they're not criminal prosecutions.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: say?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And what does the Constitution say?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It says in...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: all criminal prosecutions.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So, if you're appealing to the Constitution, you er and and and and you say it's clear language.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: I I don't think I have to argue that,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice Scalia.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: if you rely on the text of the Constitution, which is what I thought you were doing.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: I'm saying that the right to a jury trial exists because and only because it's a criminal prosecution in the Sixth Amendment.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Why? It's a criminal offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: arguing from the text...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: of the Constitution that says in all criminal prosecutions, it'd include petty misdemeanors as well as what you call major misdemeanors.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: I'm not arguing that all petty offenses are criminal prosecutions.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I know you're arguing it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: They are...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: or the- they the- they are not criminal...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: prosecutions?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: otherwise there would be a right to a jury trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: jury trial.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: has this Court held that there congruent right to a jury trial and...

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: All right. Now, before I...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: wrote an an opinion signing on to that, I'd like to see it...

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Nor has it...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I'd like everybody to have...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Nor has it held otherwise.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Fine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: we do with this case?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: then I urge the Court to at least reserve the issue of what is the appropriate right to counsel in a serious offense where, as in this case, the defendant has a constitutional right jury trial.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But the jury trial line is six months' imprisonment, isn't it?

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: And here, under Argersinger, it could be one day.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Argersinger dealt with a stop-gap issue about where there's no right to a jury trial, can we n- deny counsel, and Argersinger said no, not if you send the m- person to prison because that's the basic due process proposition.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, it doesn't follow that he could be sent to jail without providing him a lawyer in the subsequent proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I mean, in Nichols, after all, we had another conviction, and we had a lawyer representing the individual when the question came up whether the first conviction should be considered and so on.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: would...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: be a subsequent proceeding, and there would at least this is not the position he's arguing for this morning, but it would be consistent with his position that counsel be provided in that subsequent proceeding.

turns.name: STEVEN B. DUKE
turns.text: Yes, but the crucial difference is that the defendant under this proceeding that Mr. Fried is is urging the Court to approve the defendant would be sent to jail without his guilt ever being determined in a criminal trial in which he was repretented [: represented] by counsel.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Duke.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: what was the a- a- assuming that Mr. Fried's position is adopted by the Court, what is the maximum time that the Alabama trial judge could sentence the violator to? What's the maximum time in prison?

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: The suspended sentence was thirty days...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The suspended sentence of thirty da-

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: That's that's correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Can I ask you before...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: d-  d-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: There are at the bottom line of the Alabama Supreme Court has two s- phrases in it.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Then it goes to say, we tell everybody to the trial court vacate that aspect which imposed the suspended jail time.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: We're we're trying to make sense, as we understand it, of of the application of the rule of actual imprisonment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: activate the suspended sen-

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: about your I I just want to know if you agree or you disagree with their part which says we reverse that aspect...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: of the sentence.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: No no, Justice Breyer, I don't agree with that.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Let me ask you one more question.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: Were were...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Were motor vehicle cases...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: because the...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: motor vehicle cases take you out of this problem, I assume, in fine situations because if the fine isn't paid, the motor vehicle I mean, the the the the the motor vehicle laws simply provide a a a purely civil administrative means of of remedying the problem.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. PRYOR, JR.
turns.text: No, I don't.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, General Pryor.
